scores.note: It finds the load-bearing thesis cleanly: Euclid's escape from any finite list, not a tour through the page's proof catalog.
The composite-`q` branch and the overread-as-generator limit are present, but the film mostly names those boundaries instead of walking the failure the notes describe.
It reenacts the proof, but it never uses the notes' evidence hierarchy to separate what the page demonstrates from what it merely catalogs, so support and atmosphere blur together.
The numbers scene leads with a misleading 200-proof count the notes explicitly treat as catalog breadth, while the stronger quantitative bridge in scope for the notes is omitted.
It names the real alternative and costs the choice honestly: Euclid buys elementary mechanism by giving up generation, location, and density.
The close does rule favorably but narrowly, names the overclaim risk, and tells the viewer what skepticism to carry forward.

critiques.problem: "The page starts with scale: at least 200 proofs are known" plus the on-screen metric label "Proofs named by the page" turns a catalog fact into evidentiary weight, and that label is not faithful to the notes, which say only that at least 200 proofs are known.
critiques.fix: Either relabel and explicitly frame the count as breadth only, not support, or cut it and spend the quantity beat on numbers that reason from the notes: one guaranteed new prime, the factorial corollary, Bertrand's 2n window, and pi(x) ~ x / log x.

critiques.problem: "First compress the proposed list into one product" through "Either branch lands at the same destination" is a second happy-path retelling of the same proof flow, so the film spends a whole scene touring what the prior scene already established.
critiques.fix: Use that slot to interrogate the boundary the notes actually call hard: ask for the next prime and show why composite q only yields some factor outside the list, or bridge Euclid to the factorial variation so the viewer sees exactly what the theorem still proves before stronger results take over.

critiques.problem: "From any finite list, he guarantees 1 more prime outside it" jumps straight to "Bertrand-Chebyshev is already stronger," but the notes keep one missing step in scope: Euclid's immediate corollary that for every positive integer n there is at least one prime bigger than n.
critiques.fix: Insert the factorial bridge before Bertrand: form n! + 1, derive that some prime factor exceeds n, and then say why Bertrand is stronger because it locates a prime in (n, 2n) instead of merely somewhere above n.

summary: This draft is not a flat tour: it finds the theorem's real hinge and it does name a genuine trade-off. But it still misses the depth bar because the evidence hierarchy from the notes never becomes visible on screen, and the dedicated numbers scene leans on a catalog count while skipping the in-scope factorial bridge that would sharpen what Euclid does and does not prove. Revise it so the dropped hard parts from the survey become interrogation rather than setup.
